candidate: Daria Wrubel — Berkeley City Council D1
org_research: NUHW represents over 17,000 healthcare workers across California, with a core focus on behavioral health parity, safe staffing, and patient care standards. The union led the Kaiser mental health strike and is a lead sponsor of the Healthy California NOW single-payer coalition. Their endorsement questionnaire weighs labor solidarity, behavioral health expertise, and concrete municipal levers. Daria's clinical background and AFT organizing history are a direct overlap with their priorities.

questions.question: Please list any elected office you currently hold or have held previously.
questions.analysis: Honest and fine. Nothing to change.

questions.question: Please list any key volunteer organizational leadership roles you have played.
questions.analysis: Strong. The Bay Resistance Pod line is the candidate's single best immigration credential and should reappear in Section 10A.

questions.question: Please provide your official bio and include any relevant work or educational experience.
questions.analysis: Very good. The candidate's mother was a BUSD classified employee and the candidate later organized that same classified unit; one clause surfaces that labor story. One consistency note across questionnaires: this bio (high school in Oakland, college in Pennsylvania) sits against the 'lifelong Berkeley resident' framing elsewhere. Both are defensible, just pick one.

questions.question: Are you currently a union member? To which if any unions have you belonged? If you have been a union member, were you an officer, steward, bargaining committee member or activist?
questions.analysis: One of the candidate's strongest answers. Member, site rep, and paid organizer on a CFT grant. Leave it.

questions.question: What significant endorsements have you received from organizations and individuals?
questions.analysis: Jennifer Shanoski is also a longtime faculty union leader (Peralta Federation of Teachers). Naming that role makes this read as a labor endorsement, which is what this audience weighs. Group as electeds / labor / orgs as the list grows.

suggested.text:
Elected officials: Jennifer Shanoski, Berkeley School Director and Peralta Federation of Teachers leader; Soli Alpert, Berkeley Rent Board President. Additional endorsements pending. Titles listed for identification purposes only.

[Verify Shanoski's exact union title before using.]

questions.question: How much money have you raised to date? How much do you aim to raise?
questions.analysis: Good and specific. Adding contribution count and average would show grassroots depth, but not required.

questions.question: Have you conducted a poll? If yes, please provide relevant information from the findings.
questions.analysis: Fine.

questions.question: Why are you a candidate for this office? What do you believe distinguishes you from your opponent(s) as the best candidate for this office?
questions.analysis: Much stronger than the earlier draft. It now leads with the two credentials this union cares about, therapist and organizer. Solid as is.

questions.question: Briefly describe what your top legislative priorities and issue areas of focus will be if elected.
questions.analysis: For a healthcare union, a priority list with no health or labor plank is a conspicuous gap. Add behavioral health, given the candidate is a clinician and there is a live local fight (the Mobile Crisis Team closure and the county handoff).

levers.text: The council votes on Berkeley's BHSA behavioral health plan and city staffing, which are direct authority.
Housing and tenant protection run through the council and the elected Rent Board.

sources.label: Berkeleyside: Mobile Crisis Team + shelter closures

suggested.text: My priorities center on livability: increasing affordability, improving city services, preserving our educational and cultural excellence, and investing in public safety that does not jeopardize civil liberties. I would add two that reflect my own background. First, protecting our behavioral health infrastructure. As a clinician, and with Berkeley closing its Mobile Crisis Team and handing crisis response to the county, I want to make sure residents do not lose care in the transition. Second, standing with our city workers, because how we treat the people who deliver public services is how we protect those services.

verification.claim: Council votes on Berkeley's BHSA behavioral health plan and city staffing
verification.source: City of Berkeley BHSA Three-Year Integrated Plan

verification.claim: Berkeley has an elected Rent Board that handles tenant protection
verification.source: Berkeley Rent Stabilization Board (berkeleyrent.org)

questions.question: Why are you seeking NUHW’s endorsement, and what sort of support do you need from NUHW?
questions.analysis: Done and strong. 'Mental health professional' here vs 'mental health clinician' in 7A; pick one across the form.

questions.question: Do you work with organizations within your community already? If so, please name the organizations you’re closely aligned with.
questions.analysis: Good. Consider adding the Bay Resistance Pod from Background 2, and any clinical professional association (CAMFT) for this audience.

questions.question: Would you be willing to participate in any of the actions listed below. Please check-off those actions you would be willing to take. Meet with the union and the workers; Send public letters of support; Call the boss on behalf of the workers; Attend negotiations in support of the workers; Conduct facility walk-throughs to engage both workers and employers on issues; Participate in town hall meetings, campaigns and build support for union proposals in the press and with the public; Participate in picket lines, marches, rallies, and vigils; Assist in outreach to other government agencies, other elected officials and community organizations; Use the government’s power as a purchaser, contractor, or other market participant to guarantee workers’ right to organize and win fair employment conditions?
questions.analysis: Exactly right.

questions.question: Please provide any specific examples of actions listed above that you have taken to support workers in the past.
questions.analysis: Excellent, and the Kaiser detail is a direct hit. The candidate could add the AFT 6192 organizing as a first-person example, but it is already strong.

questions.question: Answer Yes or No to the below questions: 1. Do you support "card check neutrality" (remaining neutral on the question of unionization and recognizing a union as their employees’ collective bargaining representative)? 2. Do you support the employer and the union to have equal physical access and equal time to discuss workers’ choice of a union? 3. Do you support a ban on coercive kinds of communications, like "captive audience" meetings and one-on-one discussions with supervisors? 4. Do you support a short election period prior to a final and binding secret ballot vote?
questions.analysis: This is a litmus section. The extraction could not confirm which boxes are checked. Open the PDF and verify all four read Yes; a stray blank or No here is costly.

levers.text: These are yes/no support positions, not city powers. The honest answer is a clean Yes to all four.

questions.question: How would you advocate for the improvement of healthcare system standards if elected?
questions.analysis: Strong, sourced, and honest about scope. Every figure is verified below.

levers.text: Berkeley receives ~$10M/yr in state BHSA funds directly and the council votes on the Three-Year Integrated Plan. This is real authority and is separate from the general-fund deficit.
City staffing, contracting-out, and workplace safety for its own health workers are set by council-approved budgets and MOUs.
Coordination with Alameda County and Supervisor Bas is partnership, not a council power; frame as coordinated rather than duplicative.

sources.label: City of Berkeley: Mental Health / BHSA
Council agenda 4/14/26: $10M DHCS/BHSA contract (item 7)
Berkeleyside: MCT + shelter closures

verification.claim: Berkeley receives ~$10M/yr in state BHSA funds and the council votes on the Three-Year Integrated Plan
verification.source: City of Berkeley BHSA Three-Year Integrated Plan

verification.claim: City staffing, contracting, and workplace safety are set by council-approved budgets and MOUs
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority

questions.question: What if any legislation would you propose to ensure that healthcare workers, patients, and communities are protected from widespread diseases that health workers are disproportionately exposed to by virtue of their jobs?
questions.analysis: Strong and expert. Note: the Paid Sick Leave Ordinance (BMC 13.100) already exists and is generous (1 hr per 30 worked, covers anyone working 2+ hrs/wk). A good candidate answer names it and builds on it rather than proposing what Berkeley already has.

levers.text: The Cal/OSHA ATD standard (8 CCR 5199) is state law already; a councilmember's role is enforcement and speed through Berkeley's own health department, not writing it.
Berkeley's Paid Sick Leave Ordinance (BMC 13.100) is local law the council can defend and strengthen.
City procurement and employer standards (PPE, respirators, ventilation in city and contracted facilities) are directly in council control.

sources.label: CDPH: ATD standard guidance for local health depts
City of Berkeley: Workforce Standards (paid sick leave)
BMC 13.100.040: Paid Sick Leave accrual

verification.claim: Berkeley has a Paid Sick Leave Ordinance at BMC 13.100
verification.source: Berkeley Municipal Code Chapter 13.100

verification.claim: City procurement and employer standards for PPE and ventilation are council-controlled
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority

questions.question: Please list the steps you would take to maximize voter registration and participation in your jurisdiction, particularly among communities historically underrepresented in the electoral process.
questions.analysis: Good, and it now answers the noncitizen-voting prompt the question names. Ready.

questions.question: Have you led any prior policy initiatives or engaged in any other previous activities to improve behavioral health access statewide or locally? Please give details.
questions.analysis: This is the highest-leverage answer on the form and it is underbuilt. NUHW's core mission is behavioral health parity, and the candidate has a decade of direct clinical experience. Right now the concrete example is a 2014 soda tax. Keep the strong opening, then add what the candidate has actually seen: waitlists, denied authorizations, referral dead ends for young clients. No one else in this race can write those sentences. This is the one answer worth over-investing in.

levers.text: Your clinical experience is itself policy evidence. Firsthand access failures are exactly what NUHW built its Kaiser parity fight on.
Berkeley's BHSA plan is a live local vehicle you could name here as where you would direct access improvements.

sources.label: City of Berkeley: Mental Health / BHSA

suggested.text:
As a mental health clinician myself, I understand the importance of health care parity that treats behavioral health access as just as essential as all other kinds of health care, and I believe health care access is a human right. I have been in private practice as a child and adolescent therapist since 2020.

[DARIA: two or three sentences on what you have actually seen. How long do your young clients wait for care? What happens when an authorization is denied? Where do referrals dead-end? Concrete and specific.]

Locally, Berkeley is its own behavioral health jurisdiction and is writing its first Behavioral Health Services Act plan now. I would use that plan to direct funding toward the access gaps I have watched families fall through, and I would make sure the clinicians delivering that care help write it. Earlier, I worked on the 2014 Berkeley vs. Big Soda campaign, which created a funding source for health access in low-income communities.

verification.claim: Berkeley's BHSA plan is a live local vehicle for directing behavioral health improvements
verification.source: City of Berkeley BHSA Three-Year Integrated Plan

questions.question: Most of California’s local governments either purchase their own health coverage for their thousands of public employees or participate in CalPERS. Costs of public employee health plans continue increasing every year, all while provider networks continue to narrow and access to behavioral health services remains persistently challenging. What steps would you take to leverage your jurisdiction’s purchasing power or voice in CalPERS to improve access to behavioral health care?
questions.analysis: The question assumes a CalPERS relationship. Berkeley does NOT use CalPERS for health benefits — CalPERS is retirement only. Berkeley offers three HMO plans from two carriers (two Kaiser Permanente, one Sutter Health), expects to spend over $16 million on health benefits for members and dependents in 2026, and pays the full premium up to the Kaiser rate — Sutter enrollees pay the difference. The city's EAP through Claremont Behavioral Services caps counseling at 8 authorized sessions per incident per year. Coverage terms for represented employees are bargained through MOUs. The correct answer names this directly: the question's premise does not hold as stated, and the real lever is the city's direct carrier contracts.

levers.text: Berkeley offers three HMO plans from two carriers (two Kaiser Permanente, one Sutter Health), expects to spend over $16 million on health benefits for members and dependents in 2026, and pays the full premium up to the Kaiser rate. The council influences contract terms through the budget and MOU ratification.
The city's EAP (Claremont Behavioral Services) caps at 8 sessions per incident per year. Expanding that cap is a concrete negotiable item.
Berkeley's contracts with the county and service providers for crisis response set staffing and response-time standards.

sources.label: City of Berkeley Employee Benefits Guide (2026; URL filename says 2024)
Berkeleyside: MCT + county handoff (no formal agreement yet)

verification.claim: Berkeley uses CalPERS for employee health coverage
verification.source: City of Berkeley Employee Benefits Guide (2026)
verification.note: CalPERS is retirement only. Health coverage is contracted directly with Kaiser Permanente and Sutter Health. The 2026 guide is hosted at a URL whose filename says 2024.

verification.claim: Berkeley offers three HMO plans from two carriers (two Kaiser Permanente, one Sutter Health), expects to spend over $16 million on health benefits for members and dependents in 2026, pays full premium up to Kaiser rate with Sutter enrollees paying the difference
verification.source: City of Berkeley Employee Benefits Guide (2026)
verification.note: The 2026 guide is hosted at a URL whose filename says 2024.

verification.claim: EAP through Claremont Behavioral Services caps at 8 sessions/incident/year
verification.source: City of Berkeley Employee Benefits Guide (2026)
verification.note: The 2026 guide is hosted at a URL whose filename says 2024.

suggested.text: Berkeley does not participate in CalPERS for health — CalPERS is retirement only. We offer three HMO plans from two carriers, Kaiser Permanente and Sutter Health, and expect to spend over $16 million on health benefits for members and dependents in 2026. The city pays the full premium up to the Kaiser rate, with Sutter enrollees paying the difference. That is real purchasing power, and I would use it. Specifically, I would push for contract terms that require timely-access standards for behavioral health, maintain adequate in-network rosters of licensed clinicians, and resist substituting automated tools for clinical judgment. I would also work to expand our Employee Assistance Program, which currently caps counseling at eight authorized sessions per incident per year through Claremont Behavioral Services — a limit that falls far short of what many employees need. For represented employees, these terms are bargained through MOUs, and I would make behavioral health access a priority at the negotiating table. The same principle applies to our service contracts: as Berkeley shifts crisis response coordination to Alameda County, the terms of that agreement are where our standards will either hold or quietly erode, and I would fight for staffing and response-time guarantees in writing.

questions.question: Do you support the establishment of a single payer healthcare system — a publicly financed system of healthcare for all with a uniform standard of coverage that reduces costs by eliminating the profiteering, administrative waste, high prescription drug prices, and marketing expenditures that accompany private insurance? Why or why not?
questions.analysis: The yes is unambiguous and the Wicks meeting is a real credential. The question asks 'why,' and the why is missing. The candidate has one already: health care access is a human right, made specific by the candidate's clinical vantage on insurance denials.

suggested.text: Yes, I strongly support single payer healthcare, and have met with Assemblymember Wicks' office to advocate for it. I believe health care access is a human right. As a clinician, I spend real time every week on authorizations and coverage battles rather than on care, and every hour of that is an hour taken from patients. A single system would end that waste and guarantee coverage regardless of income or employment.

questions.question: If yes, how would you advocate to enact single payer solutions to our healthcare crisis?
questions.analysis: Blank. The honest answer names the limit first: a city cannot enact single payer. Saying so, then naming the real tools (resolutions, delegation lobbying, using the seat as a platform), reads as competence. The candidate has already done the lobbying piece with Wicks.

levers.text: A city cannot enact single payer. Real tools are council resolutions and lobbying the state delegation.
NUHW leads Healthy California NOW, which sponsored SB 770 (2023) toward unified financing. Naming the actual vehicle shows fluency.

suggested.text: A city council cannot enact single payer on its own, and I would rather be honest about that than promise what the seat cannot deliver. What a councilmember can do is use the platform. I would support a Berkeley council resolution backing CalCare and the unified-financing work the state began under SB 770, and I would keep meeting directly with our state delegation, as I already have with Assemblymember Wicks' office, alongside NUHW and the Healthy California NOW coalition. Local resolutions and steady pressure are how state campaigns build the momentum to win.

questions.question: What actions will you take to defend DEI and transgender care principles in your locality?
questions.analysis: Blank, and the candidate has both strong adjacent material and a unique asset: as a child and adolescent therapist the candidate almost certainly works with trans and gender-questioning young people, and gender-affirming care for minors is the exact front line under attack. California is a sanctuary state for this care under SB 107, but state law only holds if cities back it with their own resources and data practices.

levers.text: A Berkeley resolution can bar city resources, data, or staff from aiding out-of-state investigations of gender-affirming care (the model Sacramento used to strengthen SB 107 locally).
City employee health plans covering gender-affirming care without exception is a direct council commitment.
Data privacy and refusing surveillance contracts protect trans patients and providers; this connects to your existing civil-liberties stance.

sources.label: KQED: SB 107, CA as trans sanctuary state
CA Attorney General: SB 107 upheld

suggested.text:
California is a sanctuary state for gender-affirming care under SB 107, but state law only holds if local governments back it. I would support a Berkeley resolution making clear that no city resource, data, or employee will be used to help another state investigate or prosecute someone for receiving or providing gender-affirming care. I would make sure our own employee health plans cover gender-affirming care without exception, protect resident and patient data from disclosure, and refuse to let our contracts or grants be quietly rewritten to satisfy federal pressure.

[DARIA: one or two sentences from your practice on what access to affirming care means for the young people you treat. That is the sentence that will land hardest here.]

verification.claim: A Berkeley resolution can bar city resources from aiding out-of-state investigations of gender-affirming care (Sacramento/SB 107 model)
verification.source: California SB 107; Sacramento sanctuary resolution precedent
verification.note: SB 107 is state law. Sacramento passed a local strengthening resolution. Berkeley can do the same under general ordinance authority.

verification.claim: City employee health plans covering gender-affirming care is a council commitment
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority (health plan terms set through budget and carrier contracts)

questions.question: What actions would you take to promote access to culturally competent, high quality health care for communities of color and other marginalized populations?
questions.analysis: Blank. For a clinician-heavy union, the real answer is workforce: recruiting and keeping providers who reflect the community, language capacity, and reimbursement that makes it viable to practice in underserved areas. NUHW names inadequate reimbursement and workforce development in the section preamble, so echoing those shows the candidate read it.

levers.text: Berkeley's BHSA plan can direct funding toward language access and workforce pipeline for clinicians of color.
City contracting standards can require providers we fund to serve residents in the languages they speak.
Reimbursement rates are set by the state and Medi-Cal; a city advocates rather than sets them.

sources.label: City of Berkeley: Mental Health / BHSA

suggested.text:
Access that is not culturally or linguistically competent is not real access. The core of this is workforce. We cannot recruit and keep providers who reflect our communities when reimbursement rates make it impossible to practice in underserved areas and administrative burden drives clinicians out of the field. At the city level I would push for language capacity in every public-facing health service, invest in pipeline and retention support for clinicians of color, and use our contracting standards to require that providers we fund can serve residents in the languages they actually speak. Our own Behavioral Health Services Act plan is one place we can direct funding toward exactly this.

[DARIA: if you have seen culturally competent care succeed or fail in your own practice, one specific example belongs here.]

verification.claim: Berkeley's BHSA plan can direct funding toward language access and workforce pipeline
verification.source: City of Berkeley BHSA Three-Year Integrated Plan

verification.claim: City contracting standards can require language capacity from funded providers
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority (contracting)

questions.question: What ideas do you have to provide local assistance to immigrants seeking a more secure future in your city or county?
questions.analysis: Blank, but this should be one of the easiest and strongest answers. The candidate already sits on Berkeley's Sanctuary City Taskforce and co-facilitates a Bay Resistance Pod for 94702, which is rapid-response work. NUHW calls itself a sanctuary union. This suggested copy is entirely the candidate's own material.

levers.text: The council funds sanctuary infrastructure: legal defense, know-your-rights education, and city coordination with the county rapid-response network.
Refusing surveillance contracts and protecting city-held data is a direct, concrete immigrant-defense lever.
County-level legal defense (ACILEP, funded through Measure W by the Board of Supervisors) is where Berkeley plugs in and advocates for more.

sources.label: Supervisor Nikki Fortunato Bas (D5, incl. Berkeley) — immigrant rights / ACILEP

suggested.text: This is work I already do. I serve on Berkeley's Sanctuary City Taskforce, and I co-facilitate a Bay Resistance Pod for the 94702 zip code, which means being part of the network that responds when neighbors report enforcement activity. As a councilmember I would keep Berkeley's sanctuary protections strong and fund the infrastructure that makes them real: legal defense, know-your-rights education, and coordination with the county's rapid-response network. Alameda County has invested millions in immigrant legal defense and a rapid-response hotline through the ACILEP partnership, and Berkeley should be a committed partner in it, working closely with Supervisor Nikki Fortunato Bas, not a bystander.

verification.claim: Council funds sanctuary infrastructure: legal defense, know-your-rights education, rapid-response coordination
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority (budget); Berkeley sanctuary city policy

verification.claim: Refusing surveillance contracts and protecting city-held data is a council decision
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority (contracting/policy)

questions.question: Please share whether you support or oppose the cooperation of state and local governments with Federal Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officials and explain why.
questions.analysis: Blank. This needs an unequivocal one-word opening: Oppose. Then the reasoning almost no other candidate can make, that as a clinician the candidate knows what happens when people are afraid to seek care, and NUHW's own preamble is about enforcement at hospitals and schools.

levers.text: Berkeley can refuse to share data, decline surveillance contracts, and bar staff cooperation. These are concrete council decisions.
The Alameda County Sheriff has already said deputies will not aid ICE; alignment reinforces the county posture.

sources.label: Supervisor Bas / county immigrant-defense posture

suggested.text: I oppose it, without qualification. When local government cooperates with ICE, people stop going to the places that keep them safe and well. As a clinician I have seen what fear does to whether someone seeks care at all, and the federal government has made hospitals, schools, and places of worship targets. Berkeley's sanctuary protections should be enforced without exception, and that includes refusing to share data and rejecting the surveillance contracts that make sharing possible in the first place. Protecting immigrants and defending our basic civil liberties are the same fight.

verification.claim: Berkeley can refuse to share data, decline surveillance contracts, and bar staff cooperation with ICE
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority; California Values Act (SB 54)
verification.note: SB 54 provides state-level framework; local policy can go further.

questions.question: What is your definition of affordable housing and how would you work to increase the preservation and creation of housing that healthcare workers can afford near their workplaces and the communities they serve?
questions.analysis: Blank, but the candidate has more usable material here than for any other blank. Note the two halves: NUHW is asking for a definition (be concrete about AMI) and a plan. The union frame is workers priced out of the communities they serve, which connects to staffing and burnout. Adding a union-labor commitment is cheap and expected in a labor questionnaire.

levers.text: The council sets zoning, density near transit, and inclusionary/affordability requirements.
Berkeley's Rental Housing Safety Program (BMC 19.40) preserves existing habitable, affordable units through inspection and enforcement.
The council can require union labor and affordability terms on city-supported projects.

sources.label: City of Berkeley: Rental Housing Safety Program
Berkeleyside: Golden Bear supportive housing (D1)

suggested.text: I am a pro-housing and pro-tenant candidate. Affordable housing means housing a household can occupy without spending more than a third of its income on rent, defined across a real range: deeply affordable homes for our lowest-income neighbors, and middle-income housing for the nurses, therapists, teachers, and city workers now commuting in from an hour away. Responsible new housing is essential to both affordability and climate action. I support building density near transit so people can live close to where they work, preserving the affordable housing we already have through programs like our Rental Housing Safety Program, and requiring that new construction be built with union labor.

verification.claim: Council sets zoning, density, and inclusionary/affordability requirements
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority

verification.claim: Berkeley has a Rental Housing Safety Program at BMC 19.40
verification.source: Berkeley Municipal Code Chapter 19.40

verification.claim: Council can require union labor and affordability terms on city-supported projects
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority (procurement)

questions.question: Do you support rent control and the repeal of the Costa-Hawkins Act which would allow cities and counties to create policies to stabilize rents that meet their communities’ unique needs? Why or why not?
questions.analysis: Blank, and it is a litmus question that needs Yes in the first sentence. The candidate is already pro-tenant and endorsed by the Rent Board President. Berkeley has an elected Rent Board, so naming what the candidate would do through it shows knowledge of the machinery. Tie the pro-supply and pro-tenant stances together so they do not read as a contradiction: supply and protection together.

levers.text: Berkeley's elected Rent Board administers rent stabilization; the council and Rent Board can strengthen local tenant protections within current state law.
Costa-Hawkins repeal is a state question. Voters rejected repeal (Prop 33) in 2024, the third failure, so near-term the path is local while supporting future repeal.

sources.label: Ballotpedia: Prop 33 (2024) defeated
CalMatters: Prop 33 result and the local path

suggested.text: Yes to both. I am a pro-housing and pro-tenant candidate, and I do not think those are in tension: we need to build far more housing and protect the people living in the housing we already have, because building alone will not help a family priced out next month. Costa-Hawkins ties the hands of cities like ours by exempting single-family homes and newer construction and letting rents reset at vacancy. California voters have declined to repeal it three times, most recently Proposition 33 in 2024, so in the near term the path is local. That same November, Berkeley voters approved new tenant protections including a rent-increase cap, and I would work with our elected Rent Board, whose president has endorsed me, to strengthen protections here while we keep pushing for repeal at the state level.

verification.claim: Berkeley has an elected Rent Board that administers rent stabilization
verification.source: Berkeley Rent Stabilization Board (berkeleyrent.org)

questions.question: What is your approach to addressing homelessness in our community, and how will you ensure your policies both meet the immediate needs of unhoused residents and tackle the root causes of homelessness, including the lack of affordable housing and access to mental health and substance use treatment?
questions.analysis: Blank, and it is the last question they read before deciding. The prompt names mental health and substance use directly, which puts the candidate's clinical background on point, and the candidate has a completed local example in Golden Bear. Use it in past tense; it opened in 2022. This is also the right home for the housing-habitability point drafted in 5B.

levers.text: The council funds permanent supportive, transitional, and alternative housing and sets encampment policy (including whether to conduct sweeps).
Behavioral health funding through the BHSA plan and the county handoff terms directly shape the mental-health half of this question.
The Rental Housing Safety Program keeps existing units habitable, which is upstream homelessness prevention.

sources.label: Berkeleyside: Golden Bear supportive housing (D1)
Berkeleyside: MCT + winter shelter closures

suggested.text:
We must address homelessness as the most urgent symptom of our housing crisis, through permanent supportive housing, transitional housing, and alternative sites. Berkeley has shown this works: the Golden Bear on San Pablo Avenue, here in District 1, converted a motel into permanent supportive homes that have housed formerly homeless neighbors since 2022. We should be doing far more of it.

Immediate needs come first. People need shelter, sanitation, and storage now, and I do not support sweeps that take people's belongings and move them a few blocks with nowhere to go.

On root causes, housing and behavioral health are inseparable. As a clinician I know untreated mental illness and substance use both cause and follow from housing instability, and we lose people at exactly the moments they are most reachable. Berkeley just closed its Mobile Crisis Team and a winter shelter while handing crisis response to the county, and I would fight to make sure that adds up to a real system rather than a gap. Keeping existing homes safe and habitable through our Rental Housing Safety Program is part of this too, because the cheapest way to fight homelessness is to keep people housed in the first place.

verification.claim: Council funds permanent supportive, transitional, and alternative housing and sets encampment policy
verification.source: Generic charter city structural authority (budget)

verification.claim: BHSA plan directs behavioral health funding
verification.source: City of Berkeley BHSA Three-Year Integrated Plan

verification.claim: Berkeley Rental Housing Safety Program keeps existing units habitable
verification.source: Berkeley Municipal Code Chapter 19.40
